text: This will be a discovery research interview and the questions will be open-ended.
There are no right or wrong answers, so answer as honestly as you can.
Are you ready to get started?
Yes.
Yeah, because he might think you think it's funny.
Yes.
So don't let him do that to you.
Can you tell me about a specific time recently when using an ATM felt like the only or best option for you?
A time where I needed cash specifically to get into the event I was trying to attend.
Can you walk me through what happened in that specific instance, from when you realized you needed cash to when you actually got it from the ATM?
I arrived and did not know it was cash only because it was not advertised, so I had to go down the street to an ATM to pull cash out.
So you arrived at the event, found out it was cash only, and then had to locate an ATM.
How did that make you feel in the moment?
Frustrated.
Beyond the frustration, can you describe what thoughts went through your mind as you were trying to find an ATM?
That I was going to be late.
And how did you decide?
And lose my parking spot.
You were worried about being late and losing your parking spot.
How did you ultimately decide which ATM to go to?
Were there multiple options, or did you just pick the closest one?
Just picked the closest one.
And how far away was that closest ATM from the event?
1.7 miles.
You were there.
Wow, 1.7 miles is quite a distance.
So you drove to this ATM.
Did you choose a particular bank's ATM or just the first one you saw that was convenient?
Just the first one I saw because we were in the middle of nowhere and there wasn't other options.
Given that it was the only option, did you have any concerns about using that specific ATM Perhaps because it wasn't your usual bank.
I was worried about the fee, but it wasn't too terrible.
You were worried about the fee, but it wasn't too terrible.
How much was the fee?
And how did that fee impact your overall feeling about the experience?
It was like $1.75.
And then I was mad because I had to drive 15 minutes there and back out of my way to pay an extra $1.75 to get cash out.
So the fee itself wasn't huge, but the combination of the fee and the significant detour made the experience more frustrating.
Can you tell me about another situation where an ATM was the only or best option, and what led you to that decision?
The time on Marketplace where I went to buy a vehicle and the banks were closed, so I had to withdraw what I could through ATMs.
You mentioned buying a vehicle on a marketplace when banks were closed.
Can you tell me more about that situation?
What specific challenges did the closed banks present, and how did that lead you to rely on ATMs?
I was not able to pull the amount I needed out, so I had to pull what I could out and then wait for the next day to pull out the rest.
So you weren't able to get the full amount you needed for the vehicle from ATMs.
How did that impact your purchase?
Did you have to delay it, or did you make a partial payment?
[Speaker:THOMAS CARGIOLI] I had to delay it and miss work to come the next day to purchase the vehicle instead of doing it in my free time due to the banking hours.
[Speaker:INTERVIEWER] So you had to delay the purchase and miss work specifically because of the limitations of accessing cash when the banks were closed.
How did that make you feel about the situation and your banking options at the time?
Frustrated.
It's my money.
Why can't I have it on my free time?
I'd like to switch gears a bit and talk about your cash usage.
Can you tell me about a time you specifically chose to use cash instead of a card or digital payment?
Facebook Marketplace.
Can you tell me more about that Facebook Marketplace experience, specifically what made you choose cash for that transaction?
[Speaker:THOMAS CARGIOLI] There's no transaction fee or tax.
[Speaker:INTERVIEWER] That makes sense.
So the lack of transaction fees and tax motivated you to use cash for the Facebook Marketplace purchase.
Can you describe another situation where you intentionally opted for cash and explain why you chose it in that instance.
I often use cash as my budget so my bank account number doesn't change.
That's an interesting strategy.
So—
Sorry.
No worries at all.
Please continue.
I was just acknowledging your previous point.
You mentioned using cash as a budgeting tool so your bank account number doesn't change.
Can you walk me through how you implement this "cash as budget" strategy in practice?
[Speaker:THOMAS CARGIOLI] Let's say I make $100.
$80 of it goes to my bank account, I get to spend $20 of it, and that is my fun money for food, adventures, activities, etc.
[Speaker:INTERVIEWER] That's a very clear system.
So, 20% of your earnings become your fun money, in cash for things like food and activities.
How do you decide when to use that cash versus when you might still use a card for those types of purchases?
[Speaker:THOMAS CARGIOLI] If I don't have enough cash for a fun activity, then I cannot afford it.
[Speaker:INTERVIEWER] That's a very disciplined approach to managing your fun money.
So if the cash isn't there, the activity doesn't happen.
Thinking about this budgeting method, has there been a time when relying solely on the cash you've set aside for fun money presented a challenge, or a moment of unexpected inconvenience?

[Speaker:THOMAS CARGIOLI] When there were some underlying terms causing the price to go up more than I was expecting, I had to use my card and then transfer the cash into my bank to make up for that discrepancy.
[Speaker:AI RESEARCHER] So, an unexpected price increase meant you had to dip into your card and then move cash from your fun money back to your bank account to cover it.
How did that experience make you feel about your cash budgeting strategy in that moment?
[Speaker:THOMAS_CARGIOLI] Frustrated.
Not about the strategy, but more about the surprise.
[Speaker:INTERVIEWER] That's understandable.
The surprise was the issue, not the strategy itself.
Beyond these specific instances, what are your general feelings about using cash versus digital payments in different situations?
Cash is king.
"Cash is king" is a strong statement.
Can you explain what you mean by that, and in what situations you feel it's particularly true or advantageous to use cash?
I can hide purchases from the government so I'm not taxed.
That's a very clear reason for preferring cash.
You feel it offers a way to avoid taxes on purchases.
Are there any other aspects, beyond avoiding taxes, that contribute to your feeling that cash is king?
[Speaker:THOMAS CARGIOLI] If I give away a $100 bill versus swiping a card, I feel the loss.
[Speaker:RESEARCHER] That's an interesting distinction.
So, you feel the tangible loss more acutely when you hand over a physical $100 bill compared to swiping a card.
Has this feeling of loss when using physical cash ever influenced your spending habits or decisions in a significant way?
Yes, because I can visually see how much I have to spend, and when I give it away, the amount decreases.
We've been talking about your experiences using ATMs, particularly in situations where you needed cash.
Now, I'd like to shift our focus a bit.
You mentioned earlier that you only use your own bank's ATMs.
Can you tell me what makes you choose to only use Wells Fargo ATMs?
No fees.
That's it.
Can you elaborate a bit more on what you mean by "that's it"?
There is no fees when I use it versus other ATMs charge me extra money.
So, the primary reason you stick to Wells Fargo ATMs is to avoid fees.
How does it feel when you encounter a fee at an ATM that isn't your bank's?
[Speaker:THOMAS CARGIOLI] Frustrating.
[Speaker:INTERVIEWER] When you say it's frustrating, could you describe what specifically makes it frustrating for you?
[Speaker:THOMAS CARGIOLI] I feel like I'm being taxed when I'm already taxed on everything.
[Speaker:INTERVIEWER] You feel like you're being taxed again, on top of other taxes.
Has there ever been a time when the need for cash was so great that you were willing to accept that tax or fee from an out-of-network ATM?
Once, but it's not preferred.
Can you walk me through that once, when you did use an out-of-network ATM despite the fee?
What was happening at that moment that made you decide to accept the fee?
I needed cash desperately, so I had to pull the trigger.
You needed cash desperately, so you pulled the trigger.
Could you describe the situation a bit more?
What was the desperate need for cash?
I was down in the casino and I needed $100 to make my money back.
And it worked.
You were at the— It sounds like that was a high-stakes moment and it paid off.
How did you feel about that?
Sorry.
It sounds like that was a high-stakes moment and it paid off.
How did you feel about paying the ATM fee in that specific casino situation, given the outcome?
[Speaker:THOMAS CARGIOLI] Due to the current circumstances, I wasn't too worried about it, but it's still annoying.
[Speaker:INTERVIEWER] So the annoyance of the fee was still there, but overshadowed by the circumstances.
[Speaker:THOMAS CARGIOLI] Correct.
[Speaker:INTERVIEWER] When you were at that non-Wells Fargo ATM, did you notice which bank it belonged to?
And if so, did that influence your feelings about using it at all?
Not at all.
But it could in the future, just not in that situation.
So, in that specific casino situation, the bank brand didn't matter, but it might in the future.
Can you explain how the bank brand on an out-of-network ATM might influence your feelings or decision to use it in a different circumstance?
If I wasn't so caught up in the moment, I probably would not have done it, but I had exterior motives in order to go through with it.
But coming in in a clear state of mind, there's no reason for me to pay a fee to get cash out when I can do it for free through my bank.
So if you're not in a high-pressure situation, the fee avoidance is the primary driver for sticking to your own bank.
Let's think about that fee Good to hear.
I apologize, I seem to have missed part of your response.
Could you please repeat that?
Please ask the question again.
My apologies.
Let's think about that fee for a moment.
If there were no fees for using an ATM outside of your bank's network, Would you be more inclined to use them, or would you still prefer Wells Fargo ATMs?
[Speaker:THOMAS CARGIOLI] I would not have a preference at that point.
[Speaker:INDUSTRIE STREAM FINANCE RESEARCH] If there were no fees, you wouldn't have a preference.
When you use an ATM that isn't Wells Fargo, do you ever feel any sense of it being less secure, or is it purely about the fees?
[Speaker:THOMAS CARGIOLI] Typically about the fees, depending on the name of the bank.
If it's a common one I know and trust, then I'm not worried about it.
But I might have a tiny amount of anxiety if it is something that I have not come across before.
So, unfamiliar bank names on ATMs can cause a tiny amount of anxiety, even if the fee isn't an issue.
Can you describe what kind of anxiety that is?
What specific concerns come to mind when you see an unfamiliar bank's ATM?

I'm not really worried about it, but there's a small, small, small part in the back of my mind that says, "What if I'm getting scammed?" But I don't have any money, so I'm not really worried about it.
It sounds like there's a fleeting thought of being scammed with unfamiliar ATMs, but ultimately, it's not a major concern for you personally.
We've covered how fees are the main deterrent for using non-Wells Fargo ATMs, and how in a desperate situation, you'd pay the fee, though it still feels like a tax.
We've also touched on how unfamiliar bank names on ATMs can cause a slight anxiety about security, but that it's not a primary concern for you.
Given all of that, do you have any other thoughts or experiences related to using ATMs from banks where you don't have an account?
You've shared some really interesting experiences about using ATMs in specific situations.
Now I'd like to switch gears a little and talk about how your bank's digital presence, like their mobile app, compares to the experience at their ATMs.
When you use the Wells Fargo mobile app versus a Wells Fargo ATM, do you feel like you're interacting with the same bank, or do they feel like different experiences?
[Speaker:THOMAS_CARGIOLI] They feel very similar in my opinion.
Very user-friendly and easy.
[Speaker:INTERVIEWER] That's good to hear.
You mentioned both feel very user-friendly and easy.
Can you tell me what specific aspects of the Wells Fargo ATM make it feel consistent with the user-friendliness of their mobile app?
It's very bare-bones structurally to where the user-friendliness is up there.
You can withdraw, deposit.
They just keep all the options very simple and blunt, so it's very easy to understand.
It sounds like the ATM's simplicity, with its clear options for withdrawing and depositing, contributes to that user-friendly experience you also find in the app.
When you say "bare bones structurally" for the ATM, what exactly do you mean by that design choice, and how does it compare to the visual design or layout of the mobile app?
By bare bones, I mean simplicity.
I'm sure their security is very well.
They've proven to me that it is.
Compared to the mobile app, that's also very simplistic.
It's a little more complicated, but it's definitely still user-friendly.
It sounds like the simplicity in both the ATM and the mobile app is a key factor for you, even if the app has a bit more complexity.

Can you think of a specific time you've used a Wells Fargo ATM where its bare-bones design and clear options made a positive difference for you, perhaps in a situation where you were in a hurry or felt stressed?
For sure.
It takes less thinking.
So you can just tap the screen a few times and get what you need.
It sounds like the ATM's simplicity reduces the cognitive load, allowing you to complete transactions quickly and efficiently.
When you say it takes less thinking, can you walk me through an example of a time when this efficiency at a Wells Fargo ATM was particularly beneficial for you?
Winston, come here.
Not specifically, but—
That's perfectly fine.
If I hadn't had my coffee yet, it would be easier than other ATMs.
That's a great way to put it.
That even without your coffee, the Wells Fargo ATM is easier than others.
Can you describe what it is about other ATMs you've used that makes them require more thinking compared to your Wells Fargo ATM?
It's probably due to the fact that my Wells Fargo account is linked to my ATM, so it's a little more simplistic than using a third party.
That's an interesting point.
That the direct link between your Wells Fargo account and their ATM makes the experience simpler than using a third-party ATM.
Beyond just the account linking, are there any visual differences or functional aspects of a non-Wells Fargo ATM that make it feel less intuitive or consistent compared to your bank's own machines?
[Speaker:THOMAS_CARGIOLI] The software seems cheaper, and I feel like I have to go through one or two more extra steps to confirm my account.
Versus them recognizing it due to the fact that that's my home bank.

[Speaker:INDUSTRIAL STREAM FEMALE] It sounds like the software quality and the extra verification steps at non-Wells Fargo ATMs contribute to a less consistent and slightly more complicated experience for you.
When you say the software seems cheaper at other ATMs, what specific visual cues or interactions make you perceive it that way?
[Speaker:THOMAS CARGIOLI] The Wells Fargo one is more modernized, in my opinion.
That's interesting to hear your thoughts on trusting known bank names.
Now, shifting gears a bit, I'd like to explore how you see ATMs fitting into your overall banking experience, especially with so much banking being digital these days.
When you think about your mobile banking app and how convenient it is, what features or capabilities do you wish an ATM could offer that would make it feel just as modern and integrated as your app?

[Speaker:THOMAS CARGIOLI] I don't necessarily wish this, but as far as how to update it to become more technologically savvy as the app, I would say being able to see your purchases from the past, like your account statements, and then potentially check your credit score as well.
Those are interesting ideas for integrating more information into the ATM.
Could you tell me more about why seeing your past purchases or checking your credit score at an ATM would be valuable to you?
To me, it really wouldn't, but that would make it more like the mobile app, like the previous question asked.
I understand that it might not be personally valuable to you, but you brought them up as ways to make ATMs more technologically savvy, like the app.

When you think about the convenience of your mobile app, what specifically about it do you value most that you feel is missing from the ATM experience, even if you wouldn't personally use those features at an ATM?
The ability to use an ATM like a mobile app and check your account transactions.
So if you could check your account transactions at an ATM, How would that specifically improve your experience or make the ATM feel more modern and integrated with your digital banking?
[Speaker:THOMAS CARGIOLI] It would make it feel more modern and integrated, but it personally would not affect me.
[Speaker:INDUSTRIAL STREAM FINANCE RESEARCH] I understand you're distinguishing between what's generally modern and what you'd personally use.

Thinking about your own banking habits and how you use your mobile app for day-to-day tasks, Is there anything you wish you could start on your phone and then finish at an ATM, or vice versa, that would be truly helpful for you personally?
No.
Okay, I appreciate your directness.
Let's think about it from a different angle.
You've mentioned before your frustration with withdrawal limits when you needed a large sum of cash.

If an ATM could somehow connect with your digital banking profile to allow you to temporarily adjust your withdrawal limit for a specific pre-authorized transaction, would that be a feature that would make the ATM feel more integrated and useful to you personally?
Absolutely.
That absolutely tells me a lot.

Could you walk me through a specific scenario where being able to temporarily adjust your withdrawal limit through your digital banking and then completing that transaction at an ATM would have made a significant positive difference for you?
The 9 times I was trying to buy a car on a weekend.
That's a very clear example.

When you were trying to buy the car that weekend and you encountered those withdrawal limits, how would being able to preauthorize a temporary increase on your phone and then execute it at the ATM have changed that experience for you emotionally Or in terms of the practical steps you had to take?
[Speaker:THOMAS CARGIOLI] I would have been less impatient due to getting the car faster, and I would not have had to take off work that Monday to go purchase the vehicle.
[Speaker:INTERVIEWER] So, it would have saved you time, frustration, and the inconvenience of missing work.
It sounds like that one feature would have a significant positive impact on your experience with large cash transactions.

Thinking about all the things we've discussed today, from avoiding fees to the tangible feeling of cash, and now this idea of temporary limit increases— what is the single most important factor for you when choosing to use an ATM for any transaction?
